articles.title: Feathers, Fists, and Festivals: The Winners of Smithsonian's Photo Contest
articles.summary: Three baby egrets with spiky feathers that look like tiny mohawks won the grand prize in Smithsonian Magazine's 23rd annual photography contest. The photo, called "The Punk Rockers" by Jacqueline Burke, a 64-year-old veterinarian, shows the chicks squabbling under a ramp in New Jersey. Over 17,000 submissions from 108 countries were entered. Other winners include a barefoot devotee at a Hindu festival, a teenager on a horse during a Navajo coming-of-age ceremony, a colorful chameleon, a heavy Easter procession in Sicily, a circular building in China, and a Mundari child cleaning a bull's horns in South Sudan. The contest celebrates the world's beauty and diversity.

articles.title: Threads of Change: How Quechua Weavers in Peru Are Weaving a Future
articles.summary: In Peru's Sacred Valley, Quechua women have been weaving textiles for centuries. But as the world changes, they need cash for school, medicine, and transportation. A nonprofit called Awamaki, founded in 2009, helps 174 women in nine cooperatives sell their weavings and earn their own income. The women raise alpacas, shear their wool, spin it, dye it, and weave it into beautiful products. Climate change is making life harder, with melting glaciers and unpredictable weather. But Awamaki helps women earn money without leaving their communities. Weavers like Daniela and Nadia say having their own income lets them make decisions together with their families and keep their traditions alive.

articles.title: From Movie Prop to Monument: The Rocky Statue Gets Its Own Art Exhibition
articles.summary: The Rocky statue at the Philadelphia Museum of Art is one of the most visited monuments in the world. Now, a new exhibition called "Rising Up: Rocky and the Making of Monuments" explores its history and meaning. The show features over 150 works by more than 50 artists, from ancient Greek boxing sculptures to modern art by Andy Warhol, Jean-Michel Basquiat, and Hank Willis Thomas. It examines how the Rocky statue—a movie prop that became a real public art piece—represents the underdog spirit: perseverance, grit, and hope. The exhibition is organized by Paul Farber, director of Monument Lab, and includes a book with contributions from a Super Bowl champion and a film critic.
